coredata.dc:title: The isothermal section of the phase diagram of the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K

coredata.dc:creator: Jingqi, Liu
Ren, Ding
Jiqin, Qian
Yinghong, Zhuang
Jinli, Huang
coredata.dc:description: The isothermal section of the phase diagram of the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K has been investigated mainly by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), differential thermal analysis (DTA) and optical microanalysis techniques. It consists of 11 single-phase regions, 19 two-phase regions and 9 three-phase regions. At 773K, the maximum solid solubility of Ti in Mn, DyMn12, Dy6Mn23 and DyMn2 are about 8, 3, 3.5 and 3.5at% Ti, respectively. The homogeneity range of Mn2Ti extends from about 61–69at% Mn. No ternary compounds were observed in this system.

Isothermal section
X-ray diffraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The phase relationship of the Gd–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The isothermal section of the phase diagram of the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K.
Three-phase regions of the Dy–Mn–Ni ternary system
Crystallographic data of the binary compounds in the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K

article.ce:title: The isothermal section of the phase diagram of the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Materials and New Processing Technology, Guangxi University, Ministry of Education, Nanning Guangxi 530004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The isothermal section of the phase diagram of the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K has been investigated mainly by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), differential thermal analysis (DTA) and optical microanalysis techniques. It consists of 11 single-phase regions, 19 two-phase regions and 9 three-phase regions. At 773K, the maximum solid solubility of Ti in Mn, DyMn12, Dy6Mn23 and DyMn2 are about 8, 3, 3.5 and 3.5at% Ti, respectively. The homogeneity range of Mn2Ti extends from about 61–69at% Mn. No ternary compounds were observed in this system.

ce:section.ce:para: The Mn–Ti alloys are most attractive materials due to their hydrogen absorption performance and particular magnetic properties [1,2]. The alloys based on Ti such as Ti–Mn and Ti–Cr are high capacity electrode materials. There are indications that the electrode performance may be improved when Mn or Ti in Mn2Ti alloys is replaced by other elements (e.g. Zr) and the atomic radius of the other elements is larger than the atomic radius of Mn or Ti. Under this condition, the unit cell of the Mn2Ti phase expands and the stability of the stored hydrogen in Mn2Ti is improved [3]. The atomic radii of Mn, Ti, Zr and Dy are 136.6, 144.8, 159.7 and 177.5pm, respectively. If Mn or Ti in Mn2Ti alloy is replaced by Dy, the electrode performance of the Mn2Ti might be improved.

Recently, several authors found that the DyMn2 possesses excellent magnetic properties. It has been studied continuously [4–6]. When studying phase diagrams one might find new intermetallic compounds. The performance of the materials not only depends on the properties of the single-phase in the materials, but also is influenced by interaction of the various phases in the materials. The phase relationships are defined by the phase diagram. Consequently, studies of the thermodynamics of alloys and phase diagrams of alloy are an effective way in which one might find new materials and improve the original materials.
The Dy–Mn phase diagram is redrawn from Ref. [7]. Three intermetallic compounds DyMn2, Dy6Mn23 and DyMn12 were determined. The crystal structure data are from [8].

There exist different results for the intermetallic compounds in the Mn–Ti binary system. Refs. [9,10] reported on the Mn–Ti binary phase diagram. There are three intermetallic compounds in the binary system at 773K, namely αMnTi, βMnTi and Mn2Ti. Ref. [8] reported that there exist Mn15Ti85 and Mn5Ti in this system.
The Dy–Ti binary system is of a simple monotectic type [11]; no compounds form in this system [12].
The Dy–Mn–Ni, Al–Dy–Ti and Dy–Ni–Ti ternary system phase diagrams have been published previously [13–15]. Up to the present, the phase diagram of the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system has not been reported.

The purities of dysprosium, manganese and titanium used in this work are 99.9, 99.8 and 99.99%, respectively. The alloy buttons were prepared in a vacuum arc furnace under an atmosphere of purified argon. In order to ascertain the exact nominal composition in this work, some samples were prepared in a high frequency inductive furnace. The samples were sealed in evacuated quartz tubes for a homogenization heat treatment. The homogenization temperatures were chosen on the basis of the binary phase diagrams of the Dy–Ti, Dy–Mn and Mn–Ti systems. The samples, which contained less than 50.5at% Mn, were homogenized at 1053K for 15 days, and then they were cooled at a rate of 9K/h to 823K and kept at this temperature for 10 days. The samples, which contained more than 50.5at% Mn, were homogenized at 1053K for 15 days. At last all the samples were cooled at a rate of 5K/h to 773K, and kept at 773K for 15 days. Then they were quenched into liquid nitrogen.

Samples for X-ray diffraction analysis were powdered and annealed at 773K for 5 days in small glass tubes in vacuum, and subsequently quenched in liquid nitrogen. The X-ray diffraction analysis was performed with powder using a Rigaku D/Max 2500V diffractometer (a copper target, monochromator a voltage of 40kV, a current of 200mA). Powder diffraction file (PDF release 2002) was used to determine the phase relationship.
In the Dy–Ti system, we found no binary compounds and confirmed that Dy and Ti have no mutual solubility at 773K. The result obtained agrees with Ref. [12].
Three binary phases DyMn2, Dy6Mn23 and DyMn12 have been confirmed in the Dy–Mn system at 773K. Our result obtained agrees with Ref. [7].

Refs. [9,10] reported that the region between 50 and 100at% Ti does not contain intermetallic compounds at 773K and there exist a phase transition curve at 823K. No endothermal or exothermal peak in the DTA curves has been obtained in our work in this region at 823K and the intermetallic compound Mn15Ti85 has been observed by us. The intermetallic compounds αMnTi, βMnTi, Mn2Ti, Mn15Ti85 and Mn5Ti have been observed in our work at 773K. Other intermetallic compounds have not been observed at 773K.

In order to determine the solubility exactly, we prepared about 60 samples in a high frequency inductive furnace. The loss of weight is less than 0.3wt.%. The XRD analysis shows that the maximum solid solubility of Ti in Mn, DyMn12, Dy6Mn23 and DyMn2 are about 8, 3, 3.5 and 3.5at% Ti, respectively. The Mn2Ti compound was observed to have a homogeneity range. We can derive that the homogeneity range of Mn2Ti extends from about 61–69at% Mn at 773K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The phase relationship of the Gd–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the phase diagram of the Dy–Mn–Ti ternary system at 773K derived from the XRD analyses in combination with metallography. This isothermal section consists of 11 single-phase regions, 19 two-phase regions and 9 three-phase regions. The 11 single-phase regions are: α(Dy), β(Ti), γ(Mn15Ti85), δ(αMnTi), ɛ(βMnTi), ζ(Mn2Ti), η(Mn5Ti), θ(Mn), ι(DyMn12), κ(Dy6Mn23) and λ(DyMn2). The 19 two-phase regions are: α + β, β + γ, γ + δ, δ + ɛ, ɛ + ζ, ζ + η, η + θ, θ + ι, ι + κ, κ + λ, λ + α, α + γ, α + δ, α + ɛ, α + ζ, ζ + λ, ζ + κ, η + κ and θ + κ. The nine three-phase regions are: α + β + γ, α + γ + δ, α + δ + ɛ, α + ɛ + ζ, α + ζ + λ, ζ + κ + λ, ζ + η + κ, η + θ + κ and θ + ι + κ.

The components of the three-phase regions are listed in Table 1 . The XRD results confirm that eight binary compounds, namely Mn15Ti85, αMnTi, βMnTi, Mn2Ti, Mn5Ti, DyMn12, Dy6Mn23 and DyMn2, respectively, exist in this ternary system. Crystallographic data of the binary compounds are listed in Table 2 . No ternary compound was discovered.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was jointly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (Gui Ke 0447037).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pearson's Handbook, desk ed., Crystallographic Data for Intermetallic Phases

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: R.L. Beck, USAEC, LAR-10, 60 (1960) (quoted in Shunk).
